class_schedule.topic: European Integration: From War to Union
learning_objectives: Trace the history of European integration from the ECSC to the EU
Analyze the theoretical drivers: functionalism, neofunctionalism, intergovernmentalism
readings: McCormick, Understanding the European Union Ch. 1-3
Moravcsik, The Choice for Europe Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: The European Union is the most ambitious experiment in regional integration in history. Understanding how it emerged and how it works is essential for any international studies student.
key_concepts: Post-war reconciliation: Schuman Declaration and the ECSC (1951)
Treaty of Rome (1957): the common market
Single European Act (1986), Maastricht (1992), Lisbon (2009)
EU institutions: Commission, Parliament, Council, ECJ
Theories of integration: neofunctionalism vs. liberal intergovernmentalism
lecture_notes.demonstration: Institutional mapping: tracing how EU legislation is proposed, debated, and enacted

review.value: Integration in one sector creates pressures for integration in adjacent sectors (spillover), driven by supranational institutions and transnational interest groups
Moravcsik's theory that EU integration is driven by national governments pursuing domestic economic interests through intergovernmental bargaining

class_schedule.topic: The Eurozone: Monetary Union and Its Discontents
learning_objectives: Analyze the design of the eurozone and its inherent tensions
Evaluate the euro crisis and institutional responses
Assess the future of European monetary integration
readings: Brunnermeier, Harold & Landau, The Euro and the Battle of Ideas Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: The euro is the most ambitious monetary experiment since Bretton Woods. Its successes and crises reveal fundamental tensions in European integration.
key_concepts: Optimum currency area theory: why the eurozone doesn't fully qualify
Maastricht convergence criteria and the Stability and Growth Pact
The euro crisis: Greece, sovereign debt, and contagion
ECB responses: OMT, QE, and 'whatever it takes'
Banking union and fiscal integration: incomplete architecture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Crisis response exercise: simulating ECB and Council responses to a sovereign debt crisis

review.value: Mundell's theory that a single currency works best when member economies share factor mobility, economic diversification, fiscal transfers, and synchronized business cycles -- criteria the eurozone partially fails
ECB President Draghi's 2012 pledge to do 'whatever it takes' to preserve the euro, which calmed markets and marked a turning point in the euro crisis through the OMT program

class_schedule.topic: Brexit and the Limits of Integration
learning_objectives: Analyze the causes and consequences of Brexit
Evaluate what Brexit reveals about the limits of European integration
Assess the future of UK-EU relations
readings: Menon & Peet, Brexit and British Politics Ch. 1-2 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Brexit shattered the assumption that European integration was irreversible. Understanding why it happened illuminates the tensions within the EU project.
key_concepts: Euroscepticism: sovereignty, identity, immigration, democratic deficit
The 2016 referendum: campaign, result, and aftermath
Withdrawal Agreement and Trade and Cooperation Agreement
Northern Ireland Protocol: the border dilemma
Post-Brexit UK: Global Britain strategy and its limits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Post-mortem analysis: what factors best explain the Brexit vote?

review.value: The critique that EU institutions lack democratic legitimacy because key decisions are made by unelected bureaucrats (Commission) or through opaque intergovernmental bargaining (Council)
The arrangement keeping Northern Ireland in the EU single market for goods to avoid a hard border with Ireland, creating a de facto customs border within the UK -- the most contentious Brexit legacy

class_schedule.topic: European Security: NATO, Russia, and the War in Ukraine
learning_objectives: Analyze European security architecture: NATO, CSDP, OSCE
Evaluate the impact of Russia's invasion of Ukraine on European security
Assess European defense integration and burden-sharing debates
readings: Kagan, The Jungle Grows Back Ch. 3 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 fundamentally transformed European security. Understanding this transformation requires deep knowledge of the continent's security architecture.
key_concepts: NATO: collective defense (Article 5), enlargement, and burden-sharing
Russia-NATO relations: from partnership to confrontation
Russia's invasion of Ukraine: causes, conduct, and consequences
European defense: CSDP, strategic autonomy debate
Nordic and Eastern European security: Finland and Sweden join NATO
lecture_notes.demonstration: Wargaming exercise: NATO response options to a hypothetical escalation on the Eastern flank

review.value: The collective defense clause: an armed attack against one ally is considered an attack against all, invoked only once (after 9/11)
The debate over whether Europe should develop independent military capability to act without US support, intensified by concerns about US reliability and the Ukraine war

class_schedule.topic: Migration, Identity, and the Rise of Populism
learning_objectives: Analyze the 2015 migration crisis and EU policy responses
Evaluate the rise of populist and far-right movements across Europe
Assess the tension between European values and political reality
readings: Mudde, The Far Right Today Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Migration and identity have become the most politically explosive issues in European politics, fueling populist movements that challenge the liberal democratic consensus.
key_concepts: 2015 migration crisis: causes, routes, EU response
Dublin Regulation and its failures
Rise of populism: definitions, causes, manifestations
Far-right movements: National Rally, AfD, Brothers of Italy, PiS
EU migration pact: the search for a common approach
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis: populist party platforms in France, Germany, Italy, and Hungary

review.value: An ideology dividing society into 'the pure people' versus 'the corrupt elite,' demanding that politics express the general will of the people -- a thin ideology that can attach to left or right platforms
The EU rule that asylum seekers must be processed in the first EU country they enter, placing disproportionate burden on frontline states (Greece, Italy) and failing during the 2015 crisis

class_schedule.topic: The Future of Europe: Enlargement, Reform, and Global Role
learning_objectives: Evaluate EU enlargement prospects: Western Balkans, Ukraine, Moldova
Assess proposals for EU institutional reform
Analyze Europe's role in the emerging multipolar world order
readings: European Commission, Conference on the Future of Europe Report (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Europe faces defining choices: how far to enlarge, how deeply to integrate, and what role to play in a world no longer shaped by Western dominance.
key_concepts: Enlargement: Western Balkans accession process, Ukraine and Moldova candidacies
EU reform: treaty change, qualified majority voting extension, democratic legitimacy
EU as a geopolitical actor: trade power, regulatory power, military weakness
Transatlantic relations: US-EU partnership and its strains
Europe in multipolar competition: relations with China, India, Global South
lecture_notes.demonstration: Scenario planning: three possible Europes in 2040
setup_requirements: Trend data on demographics, economy, security

review.value: The process by which countries join the EU by meeting Copenhagen criteria (democracy, rule of law, market economy, adoption of EU acquis), currently active for Western Balkans and Ukraine
The 'Brussels effect': the EU's ability to set global standards through its large market, effectively exporting regulations on data privacy, competition, environment, and food safety worldwide

questions.question: Compare neofunctionalism and liberal intergovernmentalism as explanations for European integration. Which theory better explains the creation of the euro?
questions.answer: Neofunctionalism (Haas) argues integration is driven by spillover: success in one area creates functional pressure to integrate adjacent areas, driven by supranational institutions and transnational interest groups. Liberal intergovernmentalism (Moravcsik) argues that national governments, responding to domestic economic interests, drive integration through hard bargaining at major treaty negotiations. The euro can be explained by both: neofunctionalism points to spillover from the single market (a common market needs exchange rate stability, which needs monetary union), while LI points to the Franco-German bargain where Germany accepted monetary union in exchange for French acceptance of German reunification. LI arguably explains the euro's creation better because the key decisions were made at intergovernmental summits (Maastricht), but neofunctionalism better explains the subsequent pressures for banking union and fiscal integration during the euro crisis.

questions.question: What was the most significant consequence of Brexit for the European Union, and why?
questions.answer: The most significant consequence was demonstrating that European integration is reversible -- that a major member state could and would leave. This shattered the teleological assumption that integration only moves forward and gave credibility to Eurosceptic movements across the continent. Practically, the EU lost its second-largest economy, a major military power, and a key voice for market liberalism. However, Brexit also paradoxically strengthened EU cohesion: remaining members rallied together during negotiations, popular support for the EU increased in most countries as Brexit's costs became visible, and the difficulties of withdrawal discouraged other exit movements. The EU also gained policy freedom in areas where the UK had been a blocking force (defense cooperation, fiscal integration).

rubric.key: Explains why it is most significant

questions.question: How did Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 transform European security? Identify three specific changes in European defense policy.
questions.answer: Russia's invasion transformed European security by ending the post-Cold War assumption that major war in Europe was impossible. Three specific changes: (1) Germany's Zeitenwende: Germany committed EUR 100 billion in special defense spending and reversed decades of reluctance to provide heavy weapons to conflict zones, fundamentally changing European defense's largest economy. (2) NATO expansion: Finland and Sweden abandoned neutrality to join NATO, extending the alliance's border with Russia by 1,300 km and transforming Baltic security. (3) EU defense awakening: the EU activated its European Peace Facility to fund weapons for Ukraine, adopted strategic compass for defense, and began serious discussion of joint procurement and European defense industrial base strengthening.

questions.question: Define populism and explain why migration has become such a potent issue for populist movements in Europe.
questions.answer: Populism is a thin-centered ideology that divides society into 'the pure people' versus 'the corrupt elite' and demands that politics express the will of the people against elite interests. Migration is potent for European populists because it combines multiple grievances: economic anxiety (competition for jobs and public services), cultural identity (perceived threat to national culture and values from non-European immigration), sovereignty concerns (EU-mandated refugee quotas seen as overriding national control), and security fears (terrorism linked to migration, even when evidence is weak). Migration allows populists to frame the elite as out of touch with ordinary people's concerns, to cast the EU as imposing unwanted policies, and to mobilize voters on emotional identity issues that cut across traditional left-right economic divisions.

rubric.key: Explains why migration is potent for populists

major_assignments.title: European Policy Analysis Paper
major_assignments.description: Select a major European policy challenge and produce a comprehensive analysis examining its historical roots, institutional dimensions, political dynamics, and future prospects.
objectives: Demonstrate deep knowledge of European politics and institutions
deliverables: EU institutional process mapping for the issue

criteria: Demonstrates genuine command of the topic

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on European politics, institutions, and current events

participation.description: Engagement in simulations, debates, and discussions

major_assignments.description: European policy analysis paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam on EU institutions, integration theory, and key policy areas

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam with scenario-based European policy analysis

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. Simulation absences cannot be made up.
course_policies.collaboration: Simulations are collaborative. Written work individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work original with proper citation.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to EU document databases, Eurostat, European policy journals.
